title: Repugnancy & Legislative Dominance
lead: ==Art 254==: when Union and State laws on the Concurrent List produce ==conflicting results on the same facts==, the Union law prevails — with one carefully guarded exception.

items: Conflict between a Union and State law on the Concurrent List → ==Union law prevails== (Art 254(1)).

==Exception — Art 254(2)==, per [[G.C. Kanungo v. Orissa (1995)]]: State law prevails in that State if (1) a Union law exists, (2) the State enacts a ==subsequent== law on a Concurrent subject, (3) it is ==reserved for and receives Presidential assent==.
==Proviso==: Parliament may again ==supersede== such assented State legislation by a fresh law on the same matter.

items.title: Tests for Repugnancy — Deep Chand v. UP (1959)
bullets: ==Occupied field== — no apparent conflict, but both cover the same field
==Intended occupation== — Parliament meant to lay down an ==exhaustive code== replacing the State Act

items.title: Karunanidhi v. UoI (1979)
bullets: Two statutes in one field that can ==operate without colliding== → no repugnancy
A statute creating ==distinct and separate offences== in the same field → no repugnancy

items: State List interpreted ==subject to Union and Concurrent Lists== (Art 246)

sections.title: When Parliament Can Legislate on State List Subjects

head: Route
Mechanism
rows: RS passes a resolution with ==special majority== in the national interest (incl. GST) — law valid for the resolution's life; resolution lasts ==1 year, extendable 1 year== at a time.
Parliament can legislate on ==all lists== incl. State List and GST for whole/part of India.
State powers restricted ==only when Parliament actually makes== laws under 249/250 — resolutions alone don't restrict.
2+ states pass resolutions (simple majority) authorising Parliament; others may adopt later; such a law is ==amendable only by Parliament==.
Parliament may legislate on State subjects to implement ==international treaties== — no special permission needed.
President can authorise Parliament to exercise the State legislature's powers during ==President's Rule==.
Parliament legislates for UTs without legislatures; can set up courts for ==better administration of Union laws==.
State bill restricting trade & commerce needs ==prior Presidential recommendation==.

sections.title: Reservation of State Bills for the President

blocks.text: ==Art 200==: the Governor can reserve any bill for the President — a ==constitutional discretion==, not bound by State CoM advice.

items: Bills that ==should/must be reserved==: land acquisition/company mergers (==proviso to Art 31A==); bills implementing ==Art 39(b)(c)== (proviso to Art 31C); bills ==derogating from HC powers== (proviso to Art 200); Concurrent-List bills conflicting with existing central law (==Art 254(2)==); bills taxing ==water/electricity of a Parliament-created authority== (Art 288(2)); state money/financial bills during a ==Financial Emergency== if so directed (Art 360(4)(ii)).
